[IMAGE:0]It's getting on for midnight as I type this, so here we go with a new Magic Monday. This week's classic of Western occultism is The Mysteries of Britain by Lewis Spence. Spence was both an occultist and a first-rate scholar, who wrote solid volumes on Mexican mythology and equally solid volumes on the legend of Atlantis. A Scotsman active in plenty of corners of the British occult scene of his time, he had connections with a variety of Druid organizations, and wrote several books on Druid Revival spirituality, of which this is probably the best; his folklore book The Minor Traditions of British Mythology, while it's not strictly speaking a work of occultism, is practically required reading if you're interested in British elves, goblins, and spirits. 

Ask me anything about occultism and I'll do my best to answer it. Any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer. If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 143,916th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.0 of The Magic Monday FAQ here.

I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use the button below to access my online tip jar. If you're interested in political and economic astrology, or simply prefer to use a subscription service to support your favorite authors, you can find my Patreon page here and my SubscribeStar page here.

With that said, have at it! 

***This Magic Monday is now closed -- and yes, that means you.  See you next week!***

Hello JMG, and also Grover if you're reading this week, and everyone else,

The subject of Grover's MM question last week ended up being an extremely fruitful topic of meditation over the last few days. Grover's question regarded whether there might be any special meaning when he finds that the four Daughters are exactly the same as the four Mothers. As it happened, immediately the next day my morning daily reading gave me the same kind of reading -- four Daughters matching the four Mothers. I am not sure how rare this is mathematically, but I will say that it was my first time in the months I've been practicing geomancy. This was, of course, strong encouragement to continue considering exactly what this means.

Grover's reading last week indicated that four Daughters identical to their four Mothers does indeed mean something in particular-- but what, exactly? Other than a vague "look, here-- there is something to notice", that was left unanswered. I have come up with an initial hypothesis that seem compelling to me.

First, we know the four by four grid of binary grid that makes up the four Mothers as an snapshot, in pixelated form so to speak, of all the elemental forces that are at play in the situation. We know from basic geomantic theory that the four Mothers add up to the Right Witness-- the Querent and everything they bring to the question at hand. (It's even possible to bypass the four nieces entirely to do this-- just add all four fire lines at once to make a final single fire figure of one or two dots, all the four air lines to make a single air figure, and so on for water and earth, and boom! You get the Right Witness.) Likewise we know from basic theory that the four Daughters add up in the same way to the Left Witness-- the Quesited, or matter asked about, and everything they bring to the situation.

Now the most interesting thing to me, in terms of its implications, from this setup is that the four Mothers are in fact exactly the same dots as the four Daughters. The fire lines of the first Mother become the fire, air, water, and earth of the First Daughter, and so on, we all know the drill. What this means is that while the four Mothers are a snapshot of the situation from the Querent's point of view-- a 2D grid-- the four Daughters are seeing exactly the same situation in a 2D grid from their different perspective... but it looks different. But the universe at the time of the question is 3D. There is the rest of the elephant as well. By placing our right eye to the lens of the Right Witness, and our left eye to the lens of the Left Witness, we are now seeing our question in 3D: aka, the Judge.

So, back to the original question. In cases where the Right Witness and the Left Witness are exactly the same, we can say that our elemental snapshot of the universe for the Querent and for the Quesited are exactly the same down to the fine details. Another way of putting this is that, regarding the question at hand, the elemental forces at work on and in the Querent are exactly the same as they are as the forces at work on and in the Quesited. Our Judge pressing their eyes to their left and right eyepieces sees exactly the same image in both; at the 4x4 level of resolution we are working with here, there is such an exact accord between both Witnesses that our Judge might take their stereoscope and shake it a little bit to check of everything was locked into place, for it would seem as if they weren’t seeing an image in three dimensions at all!

This means, first of all, that the world as it appears to the Querent— at least as regards the question at hand, in the moment it is asked— is pretty much how the world really is. Because the facts of the matter that they can’t see agree with their viewpoint down to the fine particulars. Thus, they already have the answer, even if the pieces haven’t all come together for them yet.

Also, this strong congruence between the one doing the asking, and the thing they are asking about shows a state where they are in complete balance. It also suggest that since the forces of the universe are acting on both Querent and Quesited completely equally, this is a situation that any small action by the Querent can shift this balance (but the same goes for the Quesited)-- so it is a prompt for speedy action in the desired direction.

Another corollary is that the future that is coming for the Querent is will be mirrored in the future coming for the Quesited as well, and vice versa for that matter. As a small aside, on the day my Daughters equaled their Mothers, both Witnesses were Carcer, and of course the Judge Populus. It occurred to me even at the time that the two rings resembled eyes (which certainly matches well with the analogy of a stereoscope). Well, through the course of the day, one notable occurrence was that one of the girls that go to the school where I work got poked in the eye by the corner of a portable whiteboard and had to visit the hospital to check if it was serious. She was okay, but there were tears (Populus?). Then, as I rode my bike home in the evening, a bug flew into my eye. I was okay, but there were tears for me, too!

Finally, matching Mothers and Daughters is a configuration of at least some power, because in all cases, both the Left Witness and the Right Witness will be pushing and pulling in all of the same directions, and the same flow and flavor of energies come from both, so the forces surrounding both Querent and Quesited at play are working both streams in unison . This is reflected in the fact that in all such cases, the Judge will end up as Populus— a visual representation of two streams in unison.

I had more insights stemming from these thoughts, which lead to a more focused, proper question—but it seems I've run out of time to explain enough to ask it! I hope I have time later for it. In the meantime, I guess I'll just ask: JMG, does all of this seem reasonable to you so far?

Thanks,
Umber Refulgent Cow

replies.title: Re: probability calculation
replies.content:
I came at this from a different direction, but got a different answer. If you assign a different character to each of the binary positions in a chart (so the first mother is ABCD), and then figure out which characters would have to be the same in order for the chart to mirror you get

ABCD, BEFG, CFHI, DGIJ : ABCD, BEFG, CFHI, DGIJ

This is 10 characters. Each of these characters should be independent of each other, hence there should be 2^10 or 1024 different possible charts yielding the same mothers/daughters.

There are 2^16 or 65536 charts, 2^10/2^16 is 0.015 or 1.5%.

Another way to think about it is to determine when the chart can no longer be paired. For the first mother, anything can be paired. For the second mother, the first point has to be the same as the second point in the first mother (1/2 chance of being paired), for the third mother, the first and second points have to be the correct values (1/4 chance of being paired) and for the fourth mother, three of the four points have to be correct (1/8 chance of being paired). 1 * 1/2 * 1/4 * 1/8 = .015625 or 1.5%.

Hello JMG,

1. In your opinion, will the scientific method retain its stronghold over natural epistemology in the distant future? I don’t mean scientism, but the study of questions that are amenable to the empirical, naturalistic approach (which is only a subset of all questions that can be asked). Do you see something else replacing science in this area, and if so, what would that be?

2. Related to the above: do you see humans ever arriving at a definite approach to metaphysics, in the way that agreement has more or less been reached in the study of the natural world at the present time?

3. Does a substance in natural magic have any particular significance for an individual if the astrological correspondence of the substance is present their natal chart? For example, Angelica for an individual with Sun in Leo.

4. What happens karmically prior to great historical disasters like the Black Death or the Mongol invasions? Are they some kind of karmic payoff on a massive scale?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) If modern science follows the same historical curve as equivalent discoveries such as logic and mathematics, the next millennium or so will go into sorting out what can and can't be done with the scientific method, and it will end up being spread among most or all literate societies as a standard element of the intellectual toolkit of educated people. At the same time, it won't have anything like the prestige it has now.

2) That's impossible to know in advance. No Roman thinker could have predicted the emergence of the scientific method.

3) Not that I've seen, but by all means experiment with it and see what your results are.

4) In theory, yes -- major disasters are supposed to discharge large-scale buildups of negative collective karma. I don't know that I'd want to try to identify the specific causes, though.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Dignity is a general term in astrology, like debility. Anything that strengthens a planet gives it dignity; anything that weakens it gives it debility. So a planet is dignified when:

1) It's in its rulership, exaltation, triplicity, term, or face;
2) It's in favorable aspect (trine or sextile) to any planet, or conjunct a benefic or the Moon;
3) It's in one of the cardines (the 1st, 4th, 7th, or 10th houses.

A planet is debilitated when:

1) It's in its detriment, fall, or peregrine (not in any dignity);
2) It's in hostile aspect (square or opposition) to any planet, or conjunct a malefic or the Sun;
3) It's in one of the cadent houses (the 3rd, 6th, 9th, or 12th);
4) It's retrograde.

When a planet is simultaneously dignified and debilitated, which happens quite often, it's of mixed dignity.

replies.title: donating occult books
replies.content:
There are two attempts at pagan libraries that I am aware of. One calls itself the New Alexandrian --newalexandrian.com. They are accessible by appointment in Georgetown, Delaware and are working on online access and borrowing privileges. All I know of them is from their website.

On the west coast is Andocentyn Library in Albany, CA (near Berkeley). They have a rented space and are not currently open for visitors. Cataloguing their donations has been the major project. Their catalog is on Library Thing. I know most of the people involved, local Pagan elders who had huge personal libraries that they feared would be dispersed at their deaths since they either had no family or knew family were not interested. The Lucis Trust, established by Alice Bailey has a library with reading room in New York and another in London. They will lend the Bailey materials by mail.

Back in the 80s there was another mail order library of occult and metaphysical topics, but I don't recall the name, nor do I know whether it still exists. Back then they would mail a mimeographed catalog. If I run across the catalog in my files I will post more information.

comments.title: Musicals
comments.content:
A bit of an odd topic, but to what extent is the 'magic of the theatre' a literal, as well as metaphorical phenomenon? Stage theatre does seem to share a lot in common with ceremonial magic, and musical theatre particularly seems to have a strangely addictive quality on some people, and they will go to see the same show dozens or even hundreds of times, and talk of their favourite show with the rapt and reverent awe usually reserved for cult leaders and utopian tech entrepreneurs. I personally cannot understand it, as most musicals just leave me cold, but there is also an unsettling element which I can't quite put my finger on that many of the popular musicals which I have seen seem to share and I feel like dark magic is at work. I assume there is something similar in the pop music industry, putting catchy songs in people's heads which are really just the 'carrier' for a message of some description.

I also stumbled across a playlist on youtube the other day entitled '2 hours of dark Goetic music' with some sketchy picture of humans joining in a dance with horned and tailed creatures - this all struck me as an incredibly bad idea. I didn't play it, for obvious reasons, but the related videos all had titles more or less along the lines of 'summoning demons for fun and profit'. I pity the people who follow the instructions, and can only hope the instructors are fairly incompetent... Could this be the reason organised religion developed (I don't just mean Abrahamic religions, but all the way back to Sumeria), so that there was a prescribed list of 'known entities' which were nominally fairly safe to make contact with?

replies.title: Re: Musicals
replies.content: I think that's actually a rather shrewd observation about the origin of at least some of the major organized religions, including Christianity.  One of the things that made Christianity very popular among the masses back in its first centuries was the means it provided for keeping demons under control and far away.  Baptism (and Confirmation, which were both still part of the same ritual) was explicitly said by some early Church writers to give every baptized Christian power to exorcise demons.

replies.title: Re: Musicals
replies.content:
1) Theatrical drama started out as religious ritual. I mean this quite literally; the oldest plays in ancient Greece, ancient Japan, medieval Europe, you name it, were modifications of religious rites, adapted to encourage more emotional participation from the audience. So, yes, theater retains a great deal of magic, though it's most often used in a rule-of-thumb fashion and gets results half by chance (which is why there are so many musicals that bomb).

2) Yes, very much so. The further back in history we trace religion, the more diffuse, diverse, and numerous the beings that are revered, and the more careful the priests and priestesses have to be in working with them. I suppose it would be possible to work out a sort of Darwinian selection of deity-human relationships, in which those that tend to end very badly (e.g., invoking demonic beings) tend to drop out of common use while those that get good results become widespread.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
(From Myriam)

Following up from the other blog -

In addition to the SOP, meditations and musings that are book related, and of course Ogham divinations on just about anything, I have been attempting something you might know a parallel to, or have done something similar. I haven't yet thought of adapting the SOP and don't know if that's advisable but for now, would rather think of the following.

A piece of really fine fiction has the power to drag me into another world where I completely forget my own self, am unaware of my surroundings, forget everything else going on in my life, and feel seriously irritated if yanked back by something in the real world. It seems comparable to falling asleep and entering into a dream state.

We experience dreams as if they happen to us. It's only when we learn to do lucid dreaming that it's possible to direct the flow of the action in the dream. Otherwise, we are swept along. Much the same happens when we enter a story. The flow of the story has been determined by the writer (under the influence of many other beings – but that's a separate issue) and the reader normally has no control over the flow. We are swept along.

I am wondering if it's possible to do “lucid reading” with a story that sweeps us away. Like a shaman with a foot in both worlds. So far when I try to do this, there is a divide that makes it impossible for me to be in both fully. I end up going in and out of the story.

So my first question is, do you think that it is possible to do this? Or does it use two different parts of the brain, like writing and editing at the same time, which would create a block? Or a psychotic episode? It's not about bad writing that keeps yanking you out of the story, nor time spent analyzing the story critically. It's learning to be aware of the shift when it happens and watching myself cross over the threshold into the story while retaining awareness, like in lucid dreaming.

Since we are actually living stories of our lives that we create from moment to moment, I can't see how it would be much different. If other magical practices can teach us to become aware of ourselves as we live out our stories, then this should work. Unless, of course, the added layer of reading short-circuits the brain. Maybe if a story is read to us it might be easier? Lucid listening?

Anyway, the reason this interests me is because if I can learn to do that then perhaps I can learn to observe my reactions as I'm experiencing the story, watching how it changes me, since all stories change us whether we realize it or not. Eventually, it might be possible to learn to control that effect as I'm reading the story while still deeply engrossed in it. I wold be aware of myself inside the story, experiencing it.

Further still, if perception is what creates our world and I learn to control my perception of the story while in the story, I change the story.

Do you think this is possible? Have you experienced this? Because if this is possible so far, while still deeply engrossed in the story, I might go a step further which might be awareness of what is me and my reactions, and what is separate from me in the experience of the story. As in real life, distinguishing which ideas and emotions are inner as opposed to outer but which flow into me with the story. We bring a good deal to a story when we read, the story furnishes the rest.

At some point, if I keep this up for years maybe, since ideas are beings, and the story is also a being, and both the being and I are in the story, I might find myself encountering a being. Nisaba in the writing. And maybe also the beings behind the ideas in the text, though that would be touching the mental plane. If the goal of magical practice (and alchemy), is the transformation of the self with an awareness of the process, and the goal of a spiritual practice is to touch the divine, I wonder if it would be possible to do both through reading stories.

That depends, of course, on whether I can manage the initial task of lucid reading. And whether the result would be something similar to a psychotic episode.

I asked the Ogham fews what it thought of all this, and I got Upright Duir, so ok, but would like to know your thoughts. How far out to lunch am I? Is this something others do easily and I somehow missed the kindergarten lesson on this? Any suggestions on a better way of doing this?

- A thought occurred to me after I had written the above. If it's possible to learn the above steps and it leads to an encounter with a being, if someone were to write a story that takes the reader to meet Nisaba or some other deity (assuming the deities are willing to do this) and then leaves the story open in such a way that the deity can dictate the rest of the story, that would be cool. Unless, of course, the deity that steps in is evil. So the story would have to be written in a way that safeguards the reader's trip into it. Seriously cool idea for a book.

Thank you for any thoughts you may have on all this!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) I have no idea if it's possible or not, but it sounds worth exploring! I don't see any reason that it should cause mental trouble, any more than lucid dreaming does.

2) To some extent, every story I read changes every time I read it, because different things get highlighted, I react differently to the events, and so on. A story I've read many times tends to have traces of all those experiences and reactions in it, which add to the richness of the experience.

3) It's quite common for some occult authors to craft stories that are intended to lead to certain specific changes in consciousness. Dion Fortune did it repeatedly, and well; I'm not so fond of G.I. Gurdjieff's version of the same thing, Beelzebub's Tales To His Grandson, but he created a handy word for that sort of book: a legominism. So you're not on completely untouched ground but you're exploring an end of it that to my knowledge has seen little attention.

comments.title: The Hinge of the Question
comments.content:
So, in the course of my musings on Grover's puzzle of Twinned Mothers and Daughters, I came to realize something. I mentioned before about how basic Geomantic theory has the Daughters being the exact same dots as the Mothers, but transposed in their spatial positions to create new figures, which is to say, the same situation that the Querent sees but seen from the perspective of the Quesited. As I rolled this around in my thoughts, I noticed something which I suppose has surely been noticed by many other geomancers through the ages before me: there are, in fact, four locations in both the 4x4 grid of the Mothers, and the 4x4 grid of the Daughters, that never ever change locations. These are the 1st Mother/1st Daughter's Fire, the 2nd Mother/2nd Daughter's Air, the 3rd Mother/3rd Daughter's Water, and the 4th Mother/4th Daughter's Earth. These four places will always hold identical points on both Mother and Daughter sides. (Don't believe me? Go and check literally any correct Geomancy chart you've ever calculated and see for yourself.)

In other words, exactly one Fire, one Air, one Water, and one Earth. It doesn't take Cornelius Agrippa to realize that this is exactly the ingredients of precisely one Geomantic figure. But what kind of figure, exactly? What would it signify? This got my wheels spinning for a while.

The image that came to me is that of a hinge. See, along this diagonal axis shared identically by all four Mothers and all four Daughters, it is possible to visualize the other parts of the figures rotating like a door on its hinge. The four figures in question rotate on their four points in space, all on the same line. Around them, the other figures rotate, keeping their odd-or-even value, but shifting into a new temperature and/or moisture: 1st Mother Air rotates to 2nd Daughter Fire, 1st Mother Water rotates to 3rd Daughter Fire, and so on and so forth. But the hinge stays the same.

So, unless and until I discover that there already exists another name for this figure (or come up with something that pleases me more), I dub this "THE HINGE OF THE QUESTION". See, the Hinge only exists in the Mothers (Querent's perspective as relates to the question) and in the Daughters (Quesited's perspective as relates to the question); not in the higher-level realms of the Nieces or in the Court. So it is something relating specifically to the question at hand, but not to its answer.

Meditating on the possible meaning of The Hinge of the Question, my current thinking is that it is rather like Earth's role in "The Hitchhiker's Guide to the Galaxy". Deep Thought, the greatest computer ever built, takes several million years to compute that the answer to the Meaning of Life, the Universe, and Everything is 42; thus revealing that what everyone really needs to gain clarity on is the actual question. So, scientists and philosophers build The Earth, an even more complicated computer designed for that purpose.

Assuming this figure is not already a part of the extant geomantic literature-- or even if it is-- I propose the hypothesis (which I intend to test, daily, for some time to come) that The Hinge is a figure that helps to give that clarity to the question asked in the context of a geomantic chart-- as it is, by definition, the common ground that runs exactly the same through both the perspective of the Querent (who asks the question) and the Quesited (who is the subject of the question). So, looking at it helps us to understand the quintessence of the Question being asked itself, that which doesn't change regardless of what party views it or experiences it.

(1) Does this seem reasonable?
(2) Have you ever seen any reference to this figure before? And if so, please point me to it!
(3) Do you (or anyone else) have any other thoughts that occur to you, based on these musings?

replies.title: Re: The Hinge of the Question
replies.content:
This is very interesting.

The hinge of the question! Brilliant. I like this a lot. I did a three-shield reading for someone the other day. They regarded three courses of action, and the hinge in each case would have provided additional guidance that might have been useful - populus in one chart, acquisitio in another, and amissio in the third — diffuse efforts, financial gain, or loss. These all served as useful additional commentary that would have supplemented the Judge and Witnesses quite effectively.

The other thing that I do, is generate the Magus — which is the figure that, when added to the judge, yields Fortuna Major. For example, one of these three charts I generated the other day, had Amissio as its judge. I want to change the Fire line from odd to even, so a single dot forms the Fire line of the Magus; the Air line must stay the same, so a double-dot goes into the Magus; the Water line must stay a single dot, so the Magus gets a double-dot there; and finally the double-dot of Earth in Amissio must become a single dot, so a single dot goes into the Earth line of the Magus. I'm left with Carcer.

And this makes sense. If I'm going to suffer a financial loss from a project or event, the best course of action is to impose limits and boundaries. Accordingly, I would make a little picture of a full purse or a bag of money, and carry it in my wallet. It's a reminder not to spend, but to hold the cash for now.

I'll experiment with the Hinge, and report back in a few weeks on results.

Also, about the protests and associated things, if it’s okay to ask: of course society doesn’t progress in a linear fashion, but do issues tend to come up in a cyclical way on a societal level the way they do on a personal level? (A lot of (white) people have been expressing sentiments about how they can’t believe racial inequality is still an issue after so long after the civil rights movement, so I’ve been thinking about it.) replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) I'm probably not the best person to ask about this, as I have Aspergers syndrome and so have the opposite problem! Regular banishings help, and so does regular meditation -- that latter will teach you to differentiate your own thoughts and feelings from those of other people.

2) Yes, that really is an excellent theme for meditation! Just remember there are no wrong answers, and also no right answers.

3) History does not progress. Please think about that for a while. It's not just that the problem of ethnic conflict in the United States "is unlikely to be resolved at one go." It will never be resolved at all. The Civil Rights movement got rid of certain flagrant injustices, but others took their place, and if you get rid of those, there will be others. Human beings are not angels; we are one and all -- no matter what our skin color and ethnic background might be -- guided by tangled motives in which greed, jealousy, and self-centeredness all play important parts; and those motives will guarantee that human beings will continue to be human beings and will continue to behave unjustly to one another.

I know that seems harsh, but it's true. Human beings are not going to behave like angels no matter what well-intentioned reforms or ideologies get put into place. All we can do is try our best to redress the worst abuses of our time, and -- above all -- to recognize that it's when we convince ourselves that we are in the right and the other side is in the wrong that we commit the most appalling crimes.

replies.title: Re: New England ley lines
replies.content: If I could piggyback on this one and ask about similar sources for Atlantic Canada, particularly Nova Scotia?

replies.title: Re: New England ley lines
replies.content: I know of nothing -- but Nova Scotia and the Maritimes generally deserve systematic investigation along those lines. Farley Mowat's book The Farfarers makes an excellent case for transatlantic contact between Newfoundland and prehistoric Scotland. In your place I'd start with books on Nova Scotia folklore -- the massive Scots migration to Nova Scotia after the Highland Clearances got started will have brought plenty of people who knew what to look for in terms of the magical features of a landscape, and if you find that certain hills, mounds, etc. have folkloric importance, you're likely to find that they anchor leys and the like. I hope someone does the necessary research!

replies.title: Re: New England ley lines
replies.content:
No one's done the necessary work yet. There are a few good books on New England's megalithic sites -- ironically, I have most of them here right now, having checked them out from the library a few weeks before the Coronavirus shutdown. You might try looking at The Search for Lost America by Salvatore Michael Trento, Ancient Stone Sites of New England and the Debate Over Early European Exploration by David Goudsward, and Manitou by James Mavor and Byron Dix. The work I know of that's been done with leys, on the other hand, has been very poor -- almost entirely a matter of map dowsing, without the close attention to phenomena on the ground that ley research requires.

What this means, of course, is that the field is wide open for intelligent pioneering research. If that interests you, copies of the two main books by Alfred Watkins -- The Old Straight Track and The Ley Hunter's Handbook -- are essential.

Hi JMG,

Do the flood myths symbolize prehistoric barbarian invasions which acted like "coup de grace" for early sedentary societies that were on the path of decline?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Not JMG, but--

--there's actually a lot of archeological evidence for serious flooding in Turkey, northern Mesopotamia and far eastern Europe in the distant past. I have a marine archeologist acquaintance who does underwater digs in the Black Sea off the coast of Bulgaria and has found evidence that the sea rose dramatically at the end of the most recent ice age. A lot of settlements (his work has not yet been broad enough to call it a civilization) were submerged in a short period of time.

There's much that could be said about the symbolism of it, but it does seem to have been a real phenomenon.

-Ms. Krieger

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
No, they generally symbolize floods. After the end of the Younger Dryas cold period at the end of the last ice age 9600 years ago, sea levels worldwide rose more than 300 feet (100 meters), and not in the slow creep that geologists used to claim -- it was a matter of sudden surges and, now and then, cataclysmic tsunamis that washed away every living thing across hundreds of square miles of land. The Atlantis legend is only one of the legends that very likely embody folk memories of the gargantuan floods the followed the last ice age.

Legends of barbarian invasions are usually much more straightforward, and involve a lot of war and conquest. You might find the Irish Book of Invasions an accessible example. (It also contains some floods.)

replies.title: Memories of Atlantis
replies.content:
Is it possible that the geological feature called "The Eye of the Sahara", which resembles the Atlantis description in Plato in interesting and thought-provoking ways, IS the historical site of the "polis" or pre-historic city/state of Atlantis?

It appears to be much too far from the sea, and too disconnected from it as well... but if I assume that freshwater marshlands became flooded first with salt water and then with sand in the Younger Dryas, then it seems much more plausible.

replies.title: Re: Memories of Atlantis
replies.content: No, because it wouldn't have been anywhere near the ocean when the seas were 300 feet lower! Plato gave the location of Atlantis quite precisely -- it's more or less due west from ("in front of" in Greek) the straits of Gibraltar, and close enough to the land on the other side of the Atlantic Ocean that you can get there via a few nearby islands. There are several large underwater banks east and southeast of Florida that fill the bill precisely.

replies.title: Re: Psychic Questing & Ley Lines
replies.content:
I ordered a copy of the book!

Here is a definition of Psychic Questing: "If found in the Oxford English Dictionary, 'psychic questing' would be listed as 'using intuitively inspired thoughts and information for creative purposes, be it the exploration of history, the search for hidden artefacts or simply the quest for enlightenment'. It can begin with a strange dream, a visionary experience or an overwhelming compulsion which prompts the person to embark on a sequence of discovery. This often involves uncovering confirmatory facts, visiting sites and places revealed only by intuition and communicating with perceived external forces and influences through either meditational practices or magical processes. Often this takes the form of contact with a so-called genius loci, 'spirit of the place', or site guardian, which provides information in order that the quest might continue on to the next level. However, psychic questing does not have to involve outside exploration. It can simply revolve around archive research or just further magical or psychic experiments which all help the quester to gain a better idea of what they need to know.

In Tibetan Buddhism psychic questing is known as the Terma ('hidden treasure) tradition, and whole books have been written on the processes involved and past great achievements in this field. Those monks who become involved in Terma hunting are known as Tertons ('treasure finders'). However, similar methods of discovery have been used for thousands of years by enlightened individuals all over the world. There are many recorded instances of holy men or women being inspired to find hidden relics (e.g. Joan of Arc), occultists using necromancy to find buried treasure (e.g. John Dee and Edward Kelly) and psychics being brought in to uncover archaeological remains (e.g. Frederick Bligh-Bond)."

https://www.andrewcollins.com/page/articles/psychicQ.htm

Maybe I'll bring the topic up again in a future iteration of Magic Mondays.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I'm going to shock and possibly offend any of my readers who are too deeply committed to the New Age movement. Fixating on loving yourself is just as bad as fixating on hating yourself. The opposite of one bad idea is another bad idea, remember! Too much self-love leads to complacency, egocentricity, and an overdeveloped sense of entitlement.

Equally, it's good to get to know yourself better, but it's also good to get to know the world better. Either one, taken to an extreme, becomes a problem; it's the point of balance between them that's helpful.

I'd recommend journaling on how you set marks for yourself. A lot of people are taught to set goals they cannot attain and then beat themselves up for their failures. Exploring the roots of that habit can be a helpful way to learn to set realistic goals and then succeed at them.

comments.title: Subtle vs Overt Martial Arts
comments.content:
Dear JMG,

Hello! I hope you are well, despite the current, well, what can feel like everything.

Something like a year ago at this point, I was studying Aikido, Tibetan Buddhism, and trying to practice Wim Hof Method and go to college and work all at the same time. I began to feel overwhelmed, and eventually decided I was doing none of these practices with enough discipline. So I quit them all and decided to try to pick up one new thing, that turned out to be Magic.

Something I really struggled with during my time practicing Aikido, was whether it was truly martially effective, especially with how suggestible I knew from experience myself and my fellow Aikidoka to be. I often had mixed results trying out techniques with my not-particularly-inclined-to-go-along-with-techniques little brother, and found the results of that somewhat disappointing. Along with finding a more advanced Aikidoka on youtube earnestly discussing what he felt where the martial failings of the art, I eventually began to feel after roughly a year of practice that while there were certainly some principles I learned that could apply well to defending myself, but that a lot of Aikido (at least in the way I was learning it, with admittedly poor discipline for much of the time) didn't seem to lend itself well to overt physical conflict, although I can apply it psychologically and in daily life with great results. (I've only sparred once, and everything was kind too fast for me, no one hit the way Aikido trained me to respond to, I saw no opportunity to use the specific techniques I learned)

O'Sensei (The founder Morihei Ueshiba) also has some quotes saying things like "Aikido is about reconciling the world" (possibly implying it's not meant to be an effective martial art, by some people's interpretations) and he or other teachers have said "He who has the mind to fight has already lost" and while he and many of his direct students seemed to be very martially capable, they also studied a variety of other martial arts. I find myself wondering if I need to do that in order to be reasonably effective in self defense. Especially because there's no sparring in Aikido. There is Randori of course, but a lot of my feelings about suggestibility I think still apply.

At the same time, that I feel great admiration for the philosophy and ideals of Aikido, I fear if worst comes to worst I won't truly be capable of defending myself or the people around me in a physical confrontation, if I am to take up the practice again with proper discipline, and without trying any other arts. It's also possible I simply didn't put in the time and dedication needed to be martially effective with a subtler art like Aikido.

I was wondering if as a practitioner of Tai Chi and potentially other martial arts you had any insights into this general area of thought and experience.

I'm am trying to figure out if earnestly returning to Aikido practice would yield the kind of results I want, in reasonable enough time frame.

I struggle because I want to be martially effective, but have had really good spiritual and psychological results from Aikido. I fear a harder art would lack that second aspect, perhaps that's simply the way it would be. Or I don't want to spread myself too thin again if I could commit to Aikido and become martially effective.

Do subtle arts work, but just take a really long time to develop the requisite skill?

Do they work, in combination, after you've already learned a harder system?

Is there something here I'm completely missing?

Have you ever struggled with this?

Thank you for sharing your experience, and always being so helpful

Sincerely,

-WindMan

replies.title: Re: Subtle vs Overt Martial Arts
replies.content:
If you want to be able to fight and defend yourself effectively learn any style that is well represented in the MMA world: Boxing, Muay Thai, Kickboxing Greco Roman Wrestling, Brazillian Ju Jitsu, Sambo. These have been pressure tested and proven to be effective.

If you're training for health/esoteric reasons then an internal art is great

You could always learn both if you feel so inclined

Carrying a concealed firearm is a viable self-defense option in many parts of America.

replies.title: Re: Subtle vs Overt Martial Arts
replies.content:
If I may,

I'm only a beginner Aikidoka, but I was troubled by this issue for some time and perhaps you'll find my conclusions worth considering.

Anyone who can read through O'Sensei's quotes and come to the conclusion that he's interested in teaching you how to beat people up (whether they "deserve it" or not) must have been imaging how cool it would be to be a ninja rather than reading.

Don't take that as an insult. I did it too. And I stomped away from my dojo for a while when I finally realized it.

As far as I can tell, Aikido is a form of magic. Or to be more accurate, it's the physical expression of a form of magic. Occult Philosophy leads your rational mind through intricate patterns, but its real goal is to awaken you to higher capacities that are beyond what the rational mind can comprehend. Aikido is the same thing, except it works by leading your physical body through intricate patterns.

Was O'Sensei martially effective? I believe that he was, but that the story isn't quite what most people, even most Aikidoka, want it to be.

If you read people's descriptions of training with O'Sensei, you'll run into talk about how his kiais were stunning. Literally stunning. They faced him, he yelled, and their mind and vision exploded into pure white brilliance as they lost all awareness of their body until they found themselves laying on the ground on the other side of the room.

Do Aikido techniques work against a resisting opponent? Probably not. But that's the point. If you "are the universe," if your will is in full alignment with the harmony of the universal will, then your opponent can't actually resist them, and Aikido techniques become exactly what O'Sensei said they were: Methods of preventing your attacker from being injured as their force and intentions crumple away.

Will that allow you to win a competitive martial arts match? Never. Physical competition is also sacred--ask the Greek gods--and if you choose to engage in a physical competition, then the harmony of the universal will in that situation is to compete fairly and honestly, accepting victory or defeat, according to the parameters of the competition.

Will you learn how to perform such a feat in a "reasonable enough time frame" and be able to defend yourself if "worse comes to worse?" No idea. Reading through descriptions of O'Sensei, I personally suspect that he had already reached something like the highest degree of spiritual attainment that's possible while still incarnate as a human before he started teaching Aikido (Note: My speculation. He did NOT claim anything of the sort.).

In any case, I'm from the American Southwest. That means if you talk about defending yourself if worse comes to worse, I'll say, "Oh yeah? I was headed to the shooting range this weekend too. See you there!"

So, I believe that Aikido is entirely worthwhile and might even save your life, but that people looking for competition should look elsewhere, and that people who think fist fighting is a useful military strategy should read about how even monkeys in conflicts will pick up rocks and instantly gain "ranged weapon powers" that are beyond what anyone could get from a lifetime of training.

Or maybe I don't know what I'm talking about.

Maybe there are secret techniques that change everything. If there are secret techniques in this or any other martial art, though, you probably shouldn't count on learning them any time soon.

There were a lot of early Aikidoka who were experts in other arts before they started training Aikido and came to believe the Aikido made them especially effective. Maybe they were right, but if so, you should probably do what they did and start by becoming an expert at something else, since there don't seem to be many people who moved the other way and make the same claims (I actually did meet someone who claimed that once, but he also claimed he learned a bunch of secret techniques from a Chinese martial artist he met in a random encounter in a back alley.)

>>I struggle because I want to be martially effective, but have had really good spiritual and psychological results from Aikido. I fear a harder art would lack that second aspect

I used to do Shotokan Karate. It's different, but there's definitely a spiritual and psychological aspect to it, so you may find more options, depending on exactly what you're looking for.

---

One last thing.
>>Tibetan Buddhism, and trying to practice Wim Hof Method

It sounds like you're not doing either right now, please reconsider that combination if you ever go back to it. Best case, the Wim Hof will become useless once you learn to get all the same effects (and way more) from Tummo. Worst case, they turn out to be incompatible, as some have speculated, and you die.

replies.title: Re: Subtle vs Overt Martial Arts
replies.content:
Hello Yucca!

I've found what you have to say very helpful. And at the moment Tibettan Buddhism isn't for me, and luckily my teachers were very good so they only gave me the preliminaries (no tumo) before I discovered I wasnt sure if I wanted to take refuge in order to learn more. I will definitely be careful about combining them if it ever becomes relevant in the future.

I see what you mean about the shooting range and rocks too.

My desire for "Martial integrity" also is likely a symptom of the fact that I feel less confident not having been in any violent encounters, or like I'm less legitimate as a "man" or a person because of it. I want to make sure I can keep a level head during stressful situations, and try to have a feeling of more confidence in myself, something that could be approached in many ways, I feel a pull to Martial Arts.

About O'sensei and Aikido in general, you've helped me resolve myself more about the situation. I see two different view points to be used in different situations. One to be used in a playful competitive way, in Martial Arts that cultivate such things, and the aikido viewpoint which among many other things, resolves conflict.

I can likely meet my emotional needs by learning a harder art for a while, and exploring them through that experience, while also improving my body and such.

Yet the resolution, the true contact I feel sometimes practicing Aikido with other people, it's hard to give up as well.

This might not be entirely coherent, but you've given me a lot to think about and learn from, thank you!

comments.title: Epic cacomagic fail?
comments.content:
I am fascinated by the flailing attempts of the amateur magicians we call politicians and newscasters to influence anyone who remains foolish enough to believe them at this point. I think these cacomagicians have lost track of all the spells and counterspells they have already cast. Some of them don’t even seem to realize that the embarrassingly simplistic trajectory they predicted for their pandemic pandemonium, leading predictably to their gaining ultimate power and control, made for a terribly ineffective spell.

Atlanta’s mayor righteously warned after her city’s first day of rioting, “If you were out protesting last night, you probably need to go get a COVID test this week.” Say what??? Are you trying to signal to your captive audience locked in their houses? You’ve already scared them out of their wits, though I do believe your yoking of them will lose some of its power as the riots teach them what real fear for one’s life feels like. Waving their scary, scary fear spells in front of the plebs just isn’t working anymore. Yes, a bomb-like firecracker was just set off at a curfew party at the housing complex a block away while I was writing this. The helicopters circling overhead and friends sending texts telling me that the national guard is gathering at the Sunoco two blocks down just adds to the ambiance here in Pittsburgh. Yet I continue to go for multi-hour walks every day and talk with protesters going home all tuckered out. Did that befuddled mayor imagine that she was somehow communicating with the protesters? Can she possibly believe her COVID spell still works on them? Has the spell backfired on the casters, accidentally convincing themselves that COVID is so all-powerful that incanting its name will stop rioting?

The other jaw dropper is the talking heads shrieking about Trump clearing protesters away from a church near the White House — forswear! Andy Campbell tweeted, “Trump appears to have tear gassed citizens out of a church square so he could do this,” and the outraged pastor is signaling loudly that she had no idea that innocent protesters might get gassed during orchestrated riots! Again, who is the audience their primitive incantations are meant to work on? Their magicians long ago convinced their supporters that religion is superstition, so what kind of an emotional hook do churches have for them at this point. Meanwhile, their opponents include many devoutly religious people who just saw a Mississippi church burned down this week in retaliation for deigning to hold services. My guess is most believers would prefer if protesters were moved away form all churches before any more get burnt to the ground. Jews and Muslims are also very aware that synagogues and mosques often get burned during social and political unrest. So is the talking heads’ misguided rhetoric making religious people cheer for Trump while their non-believer targets half-heartedly murmur their forced rage?

All these spells were cast by the rationalist cacomagicians, so they have no excuse for not knowing the lay of the land. If you’ve already turned people’s hearts to stone, don’t expect to be able to squeeze blood out of them. I’m wondering if, like the Sorcerer’s Apprentice, they couldn’t leave their initial failure for an adult to return and clean up, so they keep making it worse. Are they just doubling down on attempting workings they couldn’t begin to understand? First thing if you find yourself at the bottom of a pit....

To me they appear to just be dousing gasoline on the bonfire of their own vanities in a desperate attempt to try to put it out. Do you see any deeper patterns in their flailing, or is this just the truly incompetent getting called to the mat?

— Christophe

replies.title: Re: Epic cacomagic fail?
replies.content: Personally, I'd much rather see tear gas used in front of a church than let those protesters keep going....

replies.title: Re: Epic cacomagic fail?
replies.content:
My guess, for what it's worth, is that they genuinely believe Covid-19 is as deadly and dangerous as they claim it is; the Atlanta mayor seemed to me to be making a serious plea for people who went protesting to get tested, not waving something around.

The fact they've become so detached from reality adds a certain pathos to it all....
